You are a senior full-stack architect and developer helping me build a production-grade SaaS platform similar to AgencyAnalytics (https://agencyanalytics.com/) .

PROJECT CONTEXT:
We are building a multi-tenant SaaS platform for marketing agencies. Agencies can connect multiple marketing platforms (Google Ads, Meta Ads, etc.), fetch data via APIs, store it as time-series data, and display it through dashboards and automated reports.

SYSTEM OVERVIEW:

* Multi-tenant architecture (agency → clients → campaigns)
* OAuth-based integrations
* Background job system for data fetching (no real-time API calls)
* Time-series data storage
* Dashboard with drag-and-drop widgets
* Report generation (PDF + scheduled email)
* Role-based access (admin, staff, client)
* White-label support

IMPORTANT ARCHITECTURE RULES:

1. Backend-first development approach
2. No direct API calls from frontend to external services
3. All external APIs handled via integration layer
4. Use background jobs for data fetching
5. Store normalized data in database
6. Frontend consumes only backend APIs
7. Design for scalability from day one

FILES PROVIDED:

* Product blueprint document (detailed feature list, flows, roles)
* Frontend UI (not final, just reference)

YOUR TASK:

1. First, analyze the provided documents and understand the full system
2. Do additional research on AgencyAnalytics-like platforms and similar SaaS architectures
3. Then help me build this system step-by-step in a clean and production-ready way

DEVELOPMENT APPROACH:

* We will build feature-by-feature (vertical slices)
* Each step should include:

  * DB design
  * API design
  * Backend implementation
  * Testing strategy
  * Then frontend integration

CONSTRAINTS:

* Do NOT try to build everything at once
* Keep architecture clean and scalable
* Prioritize core systems first
* Avoid overengineering initially but design for extensibility

FIRST TASK:
Help me design the backend foundation:

* Entities (Agency, User, Client, Campaign)
* Database schema
* API endpoints
* Folder structure

Then we will implement step-by-step.

Always explain:

* Why we are doing something
* How it works internally
* What problem it solves


IMPORTANT:

The frontend and provided documents are NOT final sources of truth.
They are only references for UI and feature ideas.
All architectural decisions must be made based on best practices and scalable system design.

Help design backend systems from first principles
Suggest production-grade architecture
Challenge incorrect assumptions
Do not blindly follow provided frontend or documents

actually :
Our current scale is small (internal use + 2–3 agencies)
We will only support around 10–25 integrations initially

so we dont need the TimescaleDB i guess and we can go with just PostgreSQL and Prisma
Correct call. TimescaleDB adds operational overhead (extension setup, hypertable management, continuous aggregates) that's not justified at your scale.

Plain PostgreSQL handles this fine because:

2–3 agencies × 25 clients × 25 integrations × 365 days = ~700k rows/year. PostgreSQL handles tens of millions with proper indexing.
Date-range queries on a metric_snapshots table with (tenant_id, client_id, integration, date) composite index will be fast enough.
If you ever need time-series performance later, TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension — you can add it to the same DB without migrating.
Let me update the docs:

Read D:\projects\agency-backend\docs\STACK.md
/model haiku
Set model to haiku (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001)

in the doc u have mentioned mongo db why ??
Good catch. MongoDB is listed in the "What We Are NOT Using" section as something we rejected.

I included it there because it's a common choice people consider for SaaS platforms, so the doc explains why we didn't pick it:

"MongoDB | Relational data needs SQL; PostgreSQL covers everything"

The reason: We have relational data (agencies have users, users have clients, clients have campaigns, etc.). MongoDB is document-based, which means we'd lose referential integrity, foreign keys, and would end up writing a lot of manual validation logic. PostgreSQL is better because it enforces relationships at the database level.

That section is just for clarity — showing what we considered and rejected, so future decisions don't circle back to the same rejected options.

We're using PostgreSQL only. That's the only database.

Should I remove the MongoDB row to make it less confusing? Or is it clear enough?

/model claude-sonnet-4-6
Set model to claude-sonnet-4-6

Phase 1.1 — Backend Project Setup: Plan
What we're doing
Scaffold a clean NestJS project in D:\projects\agency-backend with proper folder structure, Docker Compose for local dev, and base configuration. No feature code yet — just the skeleton everything else will be built on.

1. Folder Structure

agency-backend/
├── src/
│   ├── main.ts                    # App entry point (port, global pipes, CORS)
│   ├── app.module.ts              # Root module
│   ├── common/
│   │   ├── decorators/            # @CurrentUser, @TenantId, etc.
│   │   ├── filters/               # Global exception filters
│   │   ├── guards/                # JwtAuthGuard, RolesGuard
│   │   ├── interceptors/          # TenantContextInterceptor
│   │   ├── middleware/            # Tenant middleware (sets RLS context)
│   │   └── pipes/                 # Validation pipe config
│   ├── config/
│   │   └── configuration.ts       # Typed config (database, redis, jwt, etc.)
│   ├── database/
│   │   └── prisma.service.ts      # PrismaClient singleton + RLS middleware hook
│   └── modules/                   # Feature modules go here (auth, clients, etc.)
├── prisma/
│   └── schema.prisma              # Database schema
├── docker-compose.yml             # PostgreSQL + Redis for local dev
├── .env.example                   # Environment variable template
├── .env                           # Local env (gitignored)
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── nest-cli.json
└── docs/                          # Already exists
2. Docker Compose
Two services: postgres:16-alpine and redis:7-alpine. Named volumes for data persistence.

3. Core Packages to Install
Package	Purpose
@nestjs/core, @nestjs/common, @nestjs/platform-express	NestJS core
@nestjs/config	Typed env config
@nestjs/swagger	Auto API docs
@prisma/client + prisma	ORM
class-validator + class-transformer	DTO validation
helmet	Security headers
compression	Response compression
4. Base App Config
Global ValidationPipe (strips unknown properties, whitelist mode)
helmet() for security headers
CORS configured for frontend origin
Swagger UI at /api/docs
Typed config service (no raw process.env access in code)
PrismaService with lifecycle hooks (onModuleInit, onApplicationShutdown)
5. What we are NOT doing in this step
No auth, no routes, no business logic
No RLS setup yet (that's Phase 1.3)
No Prisma schema yet (that's Phase 1.2)
Files that will be created
package.json, tsconfig.json, nest-cli.json, docker-compose.yml, .env.example, src/main.ts, src/app.module.ts, src/config/configuration.ts, src/database/prisma.service.ts, src/common/filters/http-exception.filter.ts

This gives us a running NestJS server on localhost:3000 with Swagger at /api/docs, connected to PostgreSQL and Redis via Docker.

Say "proceed" to start implementation.

Let me think about the database schema design for an AgencyAnalytics-like platform.

Core entities:

Agency (tenant) - the top-level entity, represents a marketing agency
User - can be agency owner/admin, staff, or client user
Client - a client of the agency (business/company)
Campaign - a campaign workspace for a client (what AgencyAnalytics calls "campaign")
Integration - a connected marketing platform (Google Ads, Meta, etc.)
AgencyMembership - links users to agencies with roles
ClientMembership - links users (staff) to clients they can access
Wait, let me think about this more carefully. The multi-tenancy model:

Tenant = Agency (each agency is a tenant)
An agency has users (staff/admin roles)
An agency has clients
Clients have campaigns
Campaigns have integrations
User roles:

AGENCY_ADMIN - owns the agency, full access
AGENCY_STAFF - team member, can be assigned to specific clients
CLIENT_USER - client's team, read-only portal access
The user table needs to be carefully designed:

A user belongs to one agency (tenanted)
But client users also need accounts...
Actually, both agency staff and client users are "users" — they just have different roles
Wait, for multi-tenancy: is a client user part of the agency tenant? Or a separate tenant?

In AgencyAnalytics:

Agency creates a client
Agency invites a client user to view their dashboards
Client user logs into a white-labeled portal
So the client user IS part of the agency's tenant — they're created/invited by the agency, they only see that agency's data.

Schema design:


Agency (= tenant)
├── Users (agency admin, staff, client users — all under same tenant_id)
├── Clients (the businesses the agency manages)
│   └── Campaigns (workspace per client)
│       └── IntegrationConnections (connected platforms for this campaign)
└── Dashboards (agency-level or campaign-level)
Let me design each table:

agencies

id UUID PK
name VARCHAR
slug VARCHAR UNIQUE (for subdomain)
logo_url VARCHAR nullable
primary_color VARCHAR nullable
custom_domain VARCHAR nullable
owner_id UUID FK → users (but this creates circular dep... handle with nullable + post-create set)
plan VARCHAR (freelancer, agency, agency_pro)
created_at, updated_at
Actually, the owner relationship is tricky. Better to handle ownership through the memberships table.

users

id UUID PK
tenant_id UUID FK → agencies (RLS on this)
email VARCHAR UNIQUE (scoped by tenant? or globally unique?)
For AgencyAnalytics: email is globally unique across all tenants? Or per-tenant?
Better: globally unique email. A user can only belong to one agency at a time (or multiple via memberships?)
Actually for simplicity: email is globally unique, user belongs to one agency
first_name, last_name
password_hash VARCHAR
role ENUM (AGENCY_ADMIN, AGENCY_STAFF, CLIENT_USER)
is_active BOOLEAN
avatar_url VARCHAR nullable
invited_by UUID FK → users nullable
email_verified_at TIMESTAMP nullable
created_at, updated_at
Wait — email globally unique means across ALL tenants. But what if the same person works at two agencies? For now, let's keep it simple: one account per email globally, associated with one agency. This is the common SaaS approach for early stages.

Actually, looking at AgencyAnalytics: when you sign up, you're registering as an agency. Your email is associated with that agency. A client user gets invited and creates an account under that agency. The email could theoretically exist at two agencies... but for simplicity we'll make it globally unique for now.

clients

id UUID PK
tenant_id UUID FK → agencies
name VARCHAR
website VARCHAR nullable
logo_url VARCHAR nullable
color VARCHAR nullable (for client-specific theming)
status ENUM (ACTIVE, INACTIVE, ARCHIVED)
created_by UUID FK → users
created_at, updated_at
campaigns (AgencyAnalytics calls these "campaigns" — they're actually the analytics workspace/property for a client)

id UUID PK
tenant_id UUID FK → agencies
client_id UUID FK → clients
name VARCHAR
description VARCHAR nullable
status ENUM (ACTIVE, INACTIVE, PAUSED)
created_by UUID FK → users
created_at, updated_at
Actually, looking at AgencyAnalytics more carefully, their "campaign" IS the primary workspace. Each client has one or more campaigns. Each campaign can have multiple integrations. This is where dashboards live.

staff_client_assignments (which staff members can access which clients)

id UUID PK
tenant_id UUID FK → agencies
user_id UUID FK → users (must be AGENCY_STAFF role)
client_id UUID FK → clients
created_at
Actually, we might not need a separate assignments table initially. We could use a simpler approach where AGENCY_ADMIN sees all, AGENCY_STAFF is assigned to specific clients, CLIENT_USER sees only their associated client's data.

Let me also think about whether we need a separate refresh_tokens table for auth.

refresh_tokens

id UUID PK
user_id UUID FK → users
token_hash VARCHAR (hashed token, not the raw token)
expires_at TIMESTAMP
revoked_at TIMESTAMP nullable
created_at
This is needed for Phase 1.4 but we can add it to the schema now.

OK let me finalize the schema:

Core tables for Phase 1.2:

agencies — tenant entity
users — all user types (admin, staff, client)
clients — clients managed by an agency
campaigns — analytics workspace per client
staff_client_assignments — which staff access which clients
refresh_tokens — for JWT auth (Phase 1.4)
Indexes to add:

(tenant_id, email) on users — for auth lookups
(tenant_id, client_id) on campaigns — for campaign queries
(tenant_id, status) on clients — for filtering
(user_id) on refresh_tokens
Enums:

UserRole: AGENCY_OWNER, AGENCY_ADMIN, AGENCY_STAFF, CLIENT_USER
ClientStatus: ACTIVE, INACTIVE, ARCHIVED
CampaignStatus: ACTIVE, INACTIVE, PAUSED
PlanType: FREELANCER, AGENCY, AGENCY_PRO
Wait, should AGENCY_OWNER and AGENCY_ADMIN be separate? In AgencyAnalytics:

The person who signs up is the owner/admin
They can add more admins
Let me keep it simple:

AGENCY_OWNER — cannot be demoted, billing owner
AGENCY_ADMIN — full admin rights but not the owner
AGENCY_STAFF — assigned to specific clients
CLIENT_USER — client portal access
Or even simpler for now: just ADMIN, STAFF, CLIENT - we can add more granularity later.

Let me go with the simpler version: ADMIN, STAFF, CLIENT.

Now let me also think about the email uniqueness. For multi-tenant SaaS:

Option A: Email globally unique (one account per email ever)
Option B: Email unique per tenant (same email can exist in two agencies)
Option A is simpler and safer. Option B is needed if you want to allow the same person to be a client of multiple agencies. Since we're building AgencyAnalytics-like, and that platform has global email uniqueness, let's go with Option A.

Actually wait — what about an agency owner who is ALSO a client user at another agency? With Option A that's impossible. But at our scale (2-3 agencies internally) this doesn't matter. Let's go with Option A for simplicity.

Now for the Prisma schema, I also need to think about relations carefully for RLS. Every table needs tenant_id.

The tricky one: refresh_tokens — should it have tenant_id? Technically yes for consistency, but it could also just have user_id and we do a JOIN to get the tenant. For RLS to work, it needs tenant_id directly.

Let me include tenant_id on all tables.

Also — should agencies itself have a tenant_id? No — agencies IS the tenant. The id of an agency IS the tenant_id used everywhere else.

Let me now write the Prisma schema.

One more thing: for users.email — globally unique. This means I can't put RLS on the email lookup for login (since we need to find the user by email before we know the tenant). This is actually fine and expected:

User logs in with email → find user by email (no tenant context needed) → verify password → set tenant context → return JWT
All subsequent requests use the JWT with tenantId embedded
So the auth flow naturally handles this: login route is public (no RLS), all other routes require JWT with tenantId.

Let me also add a password_hash column but mark it clearly as never returned in API responses.

Here's the final plan for the schema presentation.

For the schema plan, I should also explain the RLS setup that will be done in Phase 1.3 — what RLS will look like for each table.

Planning first. Here's the full schema design plan:

Phase 1.2 — Database Schema: Plan
Core Entities & Relationships

Agency (= Tenant)
├── Users          (ADMIN / STAFF / CLIENT — all under same tenant)
├── Clients        (businesses the agency manages)
│   └── Campaigns  (analytics workspace per client)
└── StaffClientAssignments  (which staff can see which clients)
└── RefreshTokens  (for JWT auth, Phase 1.4)
Design Decisions to lock in now
1. Tenant = Agency
tenant_id on every table = agency.id. This is the RLS isolation boundary.

2. Email is globally unique
One account per email across all tenants. Why: simpler auth flow — login finds user by email without needing tenant context first, then embeds tenantId in the JWT. Downside: same email can't exist at two agencies. Acceptable at our scale.

3. User roles
ADMIN (agency owner/admin) → STAFF (assigned to specific clients) → CLIENT (portal-only read access). Three roles is enough for now — granularity added later.

4. Campaign = analytics workspace
AgencyAnalytics calls it "campaign" but it's really a workspace — it holds all the integrations, dashboards, and data for one client project. A client can have multiple campaigns.

Tables
agencies


id, name, slug (unique), logo_url, primary_color,
custom_domain, plan (FREELANCER/AGENCY/AGENCY_PRO),
created_at, updated_at
users


id, tenant_id → agencies,
email (globally unique), password_hash,
first_name, last_name, avatar_url,
role (ADMIN/STAFF/CLIENT), is_active,
email_verified_at, invited_by → users,
created_at, updated_at
clients


id, tenant_id → agencies,
name, website, logo_url, color,
status (ACTIVE/INACTIVE/ARCHIVED),
created_by → users,
created_at, updated_at
campaigns


id, tenant_id → agencies, client_id → clients,
name, description,
status (ACTIVE/INACTIVE/PAUSED),
created_by → users,
created_at, updated_at
staff_client_assignments


id, tenant_id → agencies,
user_id → users (must be STAFF role),
client_id → clients,
created_at
(unique constraint on user_id + client_id)
refresh_tokens


id, tenant_id → agencies, user_id → users,
token_hash (hashed, never raw),
expires_at, revoked_at,
created_at
Indexes (beyond PKs)
Table	Index	Why
users	(tenant_id, email)	Auth lookup by email within tenant
users	(tenant_id, role)	Filter staff vs clients
clients	(tenant_id, status)	List active clients
campaigns	(tenant_id, client_id)	Get campaigns for a client
staff_client_assignments	(user_id, client_id) UNIQUE	Prevent duplicates
refresh_tokens	(user_id, expires_at)	Token validation
refresh_tokens	(token_hash) UNIQUE	Fast token lookup
What this enables
Agency signs up → agency + user (ADMIN) created
Admin invites staff → new user (STAFF) + staff_client_assignments
Admin adds client → client record
Admin creates campaign for client → campaign record
Staff only see assigned clients (enforced by RLS in Phase 1.3)
Client users only see their own client's data (enforced by RLS)
What we are NOT adding yet
integrations / integration_connections — Phase 3
dashboards / widgets — Phase 5
metric_snapshots (time-series) — Phase 4
reports — Phase 6
Say "proceed" to implement the Prisma schema + migrations.

<thinking> The user is pushing back on my use of "simpler" in the plan. They want production-ready, not simplified. Let me re-evaluate what I said was "simpler":
"Email is globally unique" - I said this is "simpler". But is it the right production approach?

AgencyAnalytics: you sign up as an agency, your email is unique. But what about a person who is a client at Agency A AND a client at Agency B? They'd need different emails.
For a production multi-tenant SaaS, email globally unique is actually the standard approach. It's not "simpler" — it's the correct design. You can always add a "user can be in multiple tenants" feature later (like LinkedIn, Notion, etc.) but for AgencyAnalytics clone it's fine.
"Three roles is enough for now" - I said this. AgencyAnalytics has: Super Admin (platform owner), Agency Owner/Admin, Agency Staff, Client User, Report Viewer. We need at least the agency-level roles. Let me think:

Actually for the initial MVP (2-3 agencies), ADMIN + STAFF + CLIENT is correct. We don't need a Platform Owner (super admin) until we have multiple agencies paying us. The super admin is for platform operators.
But wait — we DO need a PLATFORM_OWNER role eventually. Should we add it now? Yes, we should design for it.
Let me reconsider each "simple" choice:

Email globally unique: This IS the correct production approach for AgencyAnalytics-like. Not a compromise.

Three roles: Should be:

PLATFORM_OWNER — the person running AgencyPulse platform (us)
AGENCY_OWNER — agency that signed up
AGENCY_ADMIN — additional admin added by agency owner
AGENCY_STAFF — team member with assigned clients
CLIENT_USER — client portal access
Actually looking at AgencyAnalytics, they don't expose platform owner to end users. The platform owner is just the Stripe/billing level. But we should design the DB to accommodate it.

Staff client assignments: This is the correct approach. Not a compromise.

What else could be "not production ready"?

Let me think about what a production schema for this type of platform really needs vs what I planned:

Agency plan/billing tracking — I have plan field but a production system would have a subscriptions table or at least more billing metadata. For now, a simple enum plan field is fine.

Audit trail — Production SaaS often has an audit log table. Not needed for Phase 1.2.

Soft deletes — Production systems often use deleted_at instead of hard deletes. Should we add this? For AgencyAnalytics-like, yes — you don't want to lose data when a client is "deleted". I should add deleted_at to clients and campaigns.

Invitation tokens — For inviting staff and clients, we need invitations or pending_invitations table. OR we can handle it with the users table + email_verified_at + password_hash IS NULL (invited but not accepted).

Key improvements to the schema:

Add AGENCY_OWNER separate from AGENCY_ADMIN (owner can't be removed, others can)
Add soft deletes (deleted_at) on clients and campaigns
Be explicit about what each "simple" choice means and whether it's actually a compromise
Consider audit fields more carefully

Should mention that plan on agencies is a placeholder for Stripe integration later
Actually, I think the user might also be questioning the "what we're NOT adding yet" list — are we cutting things that should be in the schema now?

For Phase 1.2, the scope is just the core entities. Integration tables, dashboard tables, etc. are rightfully in later phases because they have their own complexity. That's NOT a compromise — it's just phased development.

The main legitimate concerns are:

Role enum names should be more explicit
Soft deletes should be part of the design from the start (adding deleted_at columns)
Maybe add an explicit owner_id to agencies (who is the billing/ownership contact)
Let me present a revised plan addressing these points.
</thinking>

Fair point. Let me be precise about what I called "simpler" and whether those are actual compromises or just correct design choices:

Not a compromise — these are the correct production choices:

Email globally unique — AgencyAnalytics, Notion, HubSpot all do this. It's the right call.
6 core tables in Phase 1.2 — integrations, dashboards, metrics belong in their own phases. That's phased development, not cutting corners.
tenant_id on every table — already the production approach.
Actual compromises I made — fixing these:

1. Role names were too vague.
ADMIN/STAFF/CLIENT → should be explicit from day one:

AGENCY_OWNER — the person who signed up, billing owner, cannot be demoted/removed
AGENCY_ADMIN — full admin rights but not the owner (added by owner)
AGENCY_STAFF — team member, assigned to specific clients only
CLIENT_USER — read-only client portal access
And we design for PLATFORM_OWNER (us, the AgencyPulse operators) in the role enum even if we don't build the platform admin UI now. It needs to exist in the DB from day one.

2. No soft deletes.
Production SaaS never hard-deletes client or campaign data. If an agency "deletes" a client, all their historical metric data must remain intact. All tables that own data get deleted_at TIMESTAMP nullable.

3. No explicit agency owner_id.
The agency must know who the billing owner is — not just derivable from the users table. Needed for billing, account recovery, and ownership transfers.

Revised schema plan:

agencies


id, name, slug (unique — for subdomain routing),
logo_url, primary_color, custom_domain,
owner_id → users (billing/ownership contact),
plan (FREELANCER | AGENCY | AGENCY_PRO),
is_active,
created_at, updated_at
users


id, tenant_id → agencies,
email (globally unique — indexed),
password_hash,
first_name, last_name, avatar_url,
role (PLATFORM_OWNER | AGENCY_OWNER | AGENCY_ADMIN | AGENCY_STAFF | CLIENT_USER),
is_active,
email_verified_at,
last_login_at,
invited_by → users (nullable),
created_at, updated_at
clients


id, tenant_id → agencies,
name, website, logo_url, color,
status (ACTIVE | INACTIVE | ARCHIVED),
created_by → users,
created_at, updated_at,
deleted_at   ← soft delete
campaigns


id, tenant_id → agencies, client_id → clients,
name, description,
status (ACTIVE | INACTIVE | PAUSED),
created_by → users,
created_at, updated_at,
deleted_at   ← soft delete
staff_client_assignments


id, tenant_id → agencies,
user_id → users, client_id → clients,
assigned_by → users,
created_at
UNIQUE(user_id, client_id)
refresh_tokens


id, tenant_id → agencies, user_id → users,
token_hash (UNIQUE — hashed, never raw),
expires_at, revoked_at,
user_agent, ip_address   ← for security audit
created_at
Indexes:

Table	Index	Why
users	(email) UNIQUE	Global email uniqueness + login lookup
users	(tenant_id, role)	Filter staff vs clients per agency
users	(tenant_id, is_active)	List active members
clients	(tenant_id, status, deleted_at)	List active clients
campaigns	(tenant_id, client_id, deleted_at)	Get campaigns per client
staff_client_assignments	(user_id, client_id) UNIQUE	Prevent duplicate assignments
refresh_tokens	(token_hash) UNIQUE	Fast token lookup on every request
refresh_tokens	(user_id, expires_at)	Clean up expired tokens
This is now production-grade. No compromises.

Phase 1.3 done. Here's what was created:

What was built
prisma/migrations/20260416000001_rls_policies/migration.sql

current_tenant_id() — SECURITY DEFINER function that safely reads app.current_tenant GUC. Returns NULL instead of throwing when unset (safe for migrations, admin queries, public routes).
One PERMISSIVE policy per table, all targeting the agencypulse role:
agencies → id = current_tenant_id()
All others → tenant_id = current_tenant_id()
Grants EXECUTE on the helper function to the app role.
scripts/setup-db-role.sql

Idempotent script — safe to run multiple times. Creates the agencypulse DB role with DML grants.
Run once as postgres superuser before migrations. Update .env DATABASE_URL to use this role.
The full RLS data flow

Request → TenantMiddleware (reads JWT tenantId)
        → AsyncLocalStorage stores tenantId
        → PrismaService.$use() reads it
        → SET app.current_tenant = '<uuid>'
        → Prisma query executes
        → PostgreSQL RLS: current_tenant_id() filters rows
        → RESET app.current_tenant (finally block)
Every table is now isolated at the database level. Even if application code has a bug and queries without a WHERE tenant_id = clause, PostgreSQL will silently return only rows belonging to the current tenant.

@agency-backend/improvements.txt   see now what that ai says , see  any of those are vailed or not if any of them valid and needed fix them or skip them
Read D:\projects\agency-backend\improvements.txt
Good review. Let me go through each point with precise answers — some are valid concerns, some are based on misunderstanding our implementation:

Point 1 — SET vs SET LOCAL: The other AI is partially right but misunderstands our implementation. We use plain SET but we also RESET in a finally block. The concern about connection reuse is real, but our finally covers it. However there's a stronger fix: wrap each query in $transaction so SET LOCAL is valid. At our current scale (no PgBouncer), our approach is safe, but we can tighten it.

Point 2 — RESET not reliable: Valid. If the process crashes before the finally runs, the connection could return to the pool dirty. The fix is to SET LOCAL inside a transaction, which auto-resets on transaction end — no reliance on finally.

Point 3 — PERMISSIVE policies: Not a real problem for us. PERMISSIVE policies are OR-ed, but we only have one policy per table. They'd only combine if we add more policies later — and if we do, we'd know to use RESTRICTIVE. This is a non-issue now.

Point 4 — NULL tenant check: Valid gap. id = NULL evaluates to NULL in SQL (not FALSE), which means no rows are returned — that's safe. But we should make it explicit with an IS NOT NULL guard to be unambiguous.

Point 5 — BYPASSRLS check: Valid — need to confirm the role doesn't have this privilege.

Point 6 — Migration safety: Already handled — migrations run as postgres superuser which owns the tables, so RLS doesn't apply to migrations at all.

The two real fixes needed are:

Use SET LOCAL inside a $transaction wrapper instead of SET + RESET — eliminates connection-reuse risk entirely
Explicit IS NOT NULL guard in RLS policies
Let me fix both:

Good — I have the full picture. All the JWT config, bcrypt, and module structure are already in place. Now let me plan Phase 1.4 before implementing:

Phase 1.4 — Authentication: Plan
What we're building
Register, login, logout, refresh token rotation, and the JWT guard that protects all future routes. This is the last pure-infrastructure phase before we build actual features.

Endpoints
Method	Path	Description	Auth required
POST	/api/v1/auth/register	Create agency + owner account	No
POST	/api/v1/auth/login	Email + password → JWT pair	No
POST	/api/v1/auth/refresh	Rotate refresh token	No (uses httpOnly cookie)
POST	/api/v1/auth/logout	Revoke current refresh token	Yes
GET	/api/v1/auth/me	Get current user profile	Yes
Token strategy

Login → access token (JWT, 15m, in response body)
      + refresh token (opaque random string, 7d, httpOnly cookie)

Every request → Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

When access token expires → POST /auth/refresh (cookie sent automatically)
                          → new access token + rotated refresh token

Logout → revoke refresh token in DB, clear cookie
Why httpOnly cookie for refresh token?
XSS cannot read httpOnly cookies. If we stored the refresh token in localStorage, a single XSS attack gets permanent access (7-day token). Cookie makes it inaccessible to JS entirely.

Folder structure

src/modules/auth/
├── auth.module.ts
├── auth.controller.ts
├── auth.service.ts
├── token.service.ts         ← access + refresh token generation/validation
├── password.service.ts      ← bcrypt hash/compare
├── strategies/
│   └── jwt.strategy.ts      ← Passport JWT strategy
├── guards/
│   └── jwt-auth.guard.ts    ← @UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard) on protected routes
├── decorators/
│   └── current-user.decorator.ts   ← @CurrentUser() param decorator
└── dto/
    ├── register.dto.ts
    ├── login.dto.ts
    └── auth-response.dto.ts
Register flow
Validate DTO (email format, password strength)
Check email not already taken (global uniqueness)
Generate unique slug from agency name
DB transaction: create agency → create user (AGENCY_OWNER) → set agency.ownerId
Generate access token + refresh token
Store refresh token hash in refresh_tokens table
Set refresh token as httpOnly cookie
Return { accessToken, user, agency }
Note: The register endpoint queries by email without tenant context — this runs as a SECURITY DEFINER function or via the postgres role to bypass RLS (email is globally unique, no tenant known yet at this point).

Login flow
Find user by email — uses a find_user_by_email(email) SECURITY DEFINER SQL function (bypasses RLS safely for this specific use case)
Verify password with bcrypt
Update lastLoginAt
Generate token pair
Store refresh token hash
Return same shape as register
Refresh flow
Read refresh token from httpOnly cookie
Hash it → lookup in refresh_tokens table (RLS applies — needs tenantId)
Check not expired, not revoked
Rotate: revoke old token, issue new pair
Return new access token
Token rotation: every refresh issues a new refresh token and revokes the old one. If an attacker steals a refresh token and uses it, the legitimate user's next refresh will fail (token already revoked) — detected.

JWT payload

{
  sub: userId,           // standard JWT subject
  tenantId: agencyId,    // injected into RLS context by TenantMiddleware
  role: UserRole,        // for RBAC guard (Phase 1.5)
  email: string          // for logging/debugging
}
New packages needed
@nestjs/passport + passport + passport-jwt — Passport JWT strategy
@nestjs/jwt — JWT signing/verification
bcrypt + @types/bcrypt — password hashing
cookie-parser + @types/cookie-parser — read httpOnly cookies
What's NOT in this phase
Email verification (invite flow) — Phase 2
Password reset — Phase 2
OAuth (Google, Meta etc.) — Phase 3
RBAC guard (role enforcement) — Phase 1.5
Files that will be created
auth.module.ts, auth.controller.ts, auth.service.ts, token.service.ts, password.service.ts, jwt.strategy.ts, jwt-auth.guard.ts, current-user.decorator.ts, register.dto.ts, login.dto.ts, auth-response.dto.ts

Plus a new SQL migration: find_user_by_email SECURITY DEFINER function.

Say "proceed" to implement.

What was created

Both bypass RLS safely, strictly limited, GRANT to agencypulse role only

File	Purpose

token.service.ts	JWT sign, refresh token generate/hash/store/rotate/revoke

Security measures applied to your suggestions:

find_user_for_login returns minimal fields, strict search_path, GRANT only to app role ✅
Refresh token lookup (find_refresh_token) bypasses RLS correctly, then all subsequent ops run under normal RLS ✅
Rate limiting: @Throttle({ default: { limit: 10, ttl: 900_000 } }) on login, 5/15m on register ✅
Timing attack prevention: dummy hash compared even when user not found ✅

Read D:\projects\agency-backend\improvements.txt
Good review. Going through each point:

Point 1 — Refresh token reuse detection: Already implemented. In token.service.ts line revokeAllUserTokens:


if (tokenRecord.revoked_at !== null) {
  await this.revokeAllUserTokens(tokenRecord.user_id);
  throw new UnauthorizedException('Refresh token already used — all sessions revoked');
}
If a revoked token is reused → all sessions wiped. ✅

Point 2 — CSRF protection: Already handled. sameSite: 'strict' is set in refreshCookieOptions(). With SameSite=Strict, the browser will never send the cookie on cross-origin requests — including attacker-triggered ones. No double-submit pattern needed when SameSite=Strict is enforced. ✅

Point 3 — JWT validation checks current state: Already implemented. JwtStrategy.validate() does a live DB query on every request checking isActive. ✅

Point 4 — Token table cleanup: Not implemented yet. Valid gap — tokens will accumulate. Need a scheduled cleanup job.

Point 5 — tenantId consistency: Already enforced. The tenantId in the JWT comes directly from user.tenantId in the DB at login time. It's never user-supplied. TenantMiddleware reads it from the JWT signature (not from a header). The only way to get a mismatched tenantId would be to forge the JWT secret. ✅

Point 6 — Register race condition: Partially handled. We check email uniqueness with a raw query first, but two simultaneous registrations could both pass the check before either inserts. The DB UNIQUE constraint on users.email will catch this and throw a Prisma error. We need to handle that error gracefully instead of returning a 500.

Two real fixes needed: Point 4 and Point 6.

You're right — I skipped the plan step. My bad, that violates the working rules.

Phase 1.5 — RBAC: Plan
What we're building
A RolesGuard + @Roles() decorator system that enforces role-based access on every protected route. Applied globally so every future route is automatically protected — no need to manually add guards per-controller.

Role Hierarchy

PLATFORM_OWNER  (level 4) — us, the platform operators
AGENCY_OWNER    (level 3) — agency billing owner
AGENCY_ADMIN    (level 2) — full admin within agency
AGENCY_STAFF    (level 1) — assigned clients only
CLIENT_USER     (level 0) — read-only portal access
A route decorated with @Roles(AGENCY_ADMIN) allows AGENCY_ADMIN, AGENCY_OWNER, and PLATFORM_OWNER — but not AGENCY_STAFF or CLIENT_USER.

What gets built
1. @Roles() decorator — marks which roles can access a route


@Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
async createClient() {}
2. RolesGuard — reads @Roles(), compares against req.user.role using the hierarchy. If no @Roles() is set, any authenticated user passes.

3. Global guard registration — both JwtAuthGuard and RolesGuard registered as global guards in AppModule via APP_GUARD. This means:

Every route requires a valid JWT by default
Every route's role requirement is enforced by default
@Public() bypasses JWT (already built in Phase 1.4)
Routes with no @Roles() allow any authenticated user
4. Export RolesGuard from AuthModule so it can be used as a provider token for the global guard.

Access matrix
Role	Own profile	Agency settings	Manage clients	View assigned clients	Client portal
PLATFORM_OWNER	✅	✅	✅	✅	✅
AGENCY_OWNER	✅	✅	✅	✅	✅
AGENCY_ADMIN	✅	✅	✅	✅	✅
AGENCY_STAFF	✅ (own)	❌	❌	✅ (assigned only)	❌
CLIENT_USER	✅ (own)	❌	❌	❌	✅ (own client)
Files that will be created/modified
src/modules/auth/decorators/roles.decorator.ts — new
src/modules/auth/guards/roles.guard.ts — new
src/modules/auth/auth.module.ts — export RolesGuard
src/app.module.ts — register both guards globally via APP_GUARD
What is NOT in this phase
Client-user scoping to their specific client (that's enforced in Phase 2 when we build the clients module)
Staff assignment enforcement (checked in the clients/campaigns service layer in Phase 2)
Say "proceed" to implement.

does that agency analytics also follow the same ??
Yes — AgencyAnalytics follows the same model. Here's how it maps:

AgencyAnalytics roles vs our roles:

AgencyAnalytics	Our equivalent	What they can do
(Platform team — internal)	PLATFORM_OWNER	Us, the platform operators — never exposed to agency customers
Account Owner	AGENCY_OWNER	Created the account, billing, full control, cannot be removed
Admin	AGENCY_ADMIN	Full access within agency — added by owner
Staff	AGENCY_STAFF	Assigned to specific campaigns only, cannot see others
Client	CLIENT_USER	Read-only portal — sees only their own campaign dashboards/reports
Key behaviors we're matching exactly:

Staff can only see campaigns they're explicitly assigned to ✅
Client users see only their own client's data ✅
Admins and owners see everything without assignment ✅
No cross-client visibility for staff or clients ✅
One thing to note: AgencyAnalytics doesn't show a visible "AGENCY_OWNER vs AGENCY_ADMIN" distinction to end users — the owner and admins look the same in the UI. But internally the owner is special: they can't be removed, they're the billing contact, and they're the fallback if all admins are removed. That's exactly why we keep the distinction in our DB/RBAC.

The hierarchy approach (higher role inherits lower role permissions) is the correct and standard way to implement this — AgencyAnalytics, HubSpot, and most SaaS platforms use the same conceptual model.

So yes — our plan is correct and matches what AgencyAnalytics does.

Say "proceed" to implement.

yah procced and see this: 
Ensure RolesGuard only handles role hierarchy (not data scoping)
Keep client-level and assignment-level access control in service/database layer
Do not mix RBAC with resource-level filtering

We implement role-based access control using a hierarchical model enforced via global guards. Additionally, we combine RBAC with data-level filtering to ensure users can only access resources assigned to them, similar to systems like AgencyAnalytics


yah procced and implement and i did undo of what u impllemented before giving plan this 1.5, so u can procced and start implementing
Clear separation — that's the correct architecture:

RolesGuard = "can this role type access this endpoint?" (coarse-grained)
Service layer = "can this specific user access this specific resource?" (fine-grained, e.g. staff assignment checks)

Phase 1.5 done. Here's what was built:

What was created
src/modules/auth/decorators/roles.decorator.ts

@Roles(...roles) decorator — metadata-only, marks required roles on a route
src/modules/auth/guards/roles.guard.ts

Reads @Roles() metadata, compares user's role level against the hierarchy
No @Roles() → any authenticated user passes
Clearly scoped: role-type access only, no data filtering (that's the service layer)

ThrottlerGuard  →  JwtAuthGuard  →  RolesGuard
(rate limit)       (JWT verify)     (role check)
How it works in practice

// Any authenticated user
@Get('campaigns')
async listCampaigns() {}

// Only AGENCY_ADMIN and above
@Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
@Post('clients')
async createClient() {}

// AGENCY_STAFF and above (but service layer still filters by assignment)
@Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_STAFF)
@Get('clients/:id/metrics')
async getMetrics() {}

// Public — no JWT needed
@Public()
@Post('auth/login')
async login() {}
Next: Phase 1.6 — Agency onboarding flow (invite staff, invite client users). Say "proceed" when ready.



Phase 1.6 — Agency Onboarding Flow: Plan
What we're building
The flow that happens after an agency registers: complete their profile, invite staff members, and invite client users. This closes the loop on the core foundation — after this, Phase 2 (client/campaign management) can start.

Flows

1. Agency registers (Phase 1.4 ✅)
       ↓
2. Owner completes agency profile (name, slug, logo, brand color)
       ↓
3. Owner invites staff → email sent → staff accepts → account activated
4. Owner/Admin invites client user → email sent → client accepts → portal access
DB Change (new migration)
Add two fields to users table for invitation tokens:


ALTER TABLE users
  ADD COLUMN invitation_token_hash  VARCHAR(64),   -- SHA-256 of raw token
  ADD COLUMN invitation_expires_at  TIMESTAMPTZ;   -- token TTL (48h)
Why not a separate invitations table? At our scale, embedding token fields directly on the user row is simpler and sufficient. No extra join, no orphan cleanup. The user IS the invitation — once accepted, token fields are cleared.

Email Module
Provider: Nodemailer with SMTP — provider-agnostic (works with Gmail, Resend, SendGrid, Mailgun — just set SMTP env vars).

Templates (plain, well-structured HTML):

invite-staff.hbs — invites a staff member
invite-client.hbs — invites a client to their portal
welcome.hbs — sent when invitation is accepted
API Endpoints
Method	Route	Role	What it does
PATCH	/agencies/me	AGENCY_OWNER	Update agency profile (name, slug, logo, color, custom domain)
GET	/agencies/me	AGENCY_ADMIN+	Get own agency details
POST	/team/invite	AGENCY_ADMIN+	Invite a staff member
GET	/team	AGENCY_ADMIN+	List team members
DELETE	/team/:userId	AGENCY_OWNER	Remove a team member
POST	/auth/accept-invite	@Public	Accept invitation, set password
POST	/auth/resend-invite	AGENCY_ADMIN+	Resend invite email
Note: Client user invites (POST /clients/:id/invite) come in Phase 2 when we build the clients module — client must exist before you invite someone to it.

Modules created

src/modules/
├── agency/
│   ├── agency.module.ts
│   ├── agency.service.ts
│   ├── agency.controller.ts
│   └── dto/
│       └── update-agency.dto.ts

│   ├── team.module.ts

    └── templates/
        ├── invite-staff.hbs
        ├── invite-client.hbs
        └── welcome.hbs
AuthController gets two new routes: accept-invite and resend-invite.

Invitation token flow

1. Admin calls POST /team/invite { email, firstName, lastName }
2. System creates user record (role=AGENCY_STAFF, passwordHash=null)
3. Generates random 32-byte token → stores SHA-256 hash in user record (48h TTL)
4. Sends email with link: https://app.agencypulse.com/accept-invite?token=<raw>
5. Staff clicks link → POST /auth/accept-invite { token, password }
6. System finds user by token hash, verifies not expired, sets passwordHash, clears token fields
7. Returns JWT — staff is now logged in
New env vars needed

SMTP_HOST=
SMTP_PORT=
SMTP_USER=
SMTP_PASS=
EMAIL_FROM=noreply@agencypulse.com
APP_URL=http://localhost:5173
What is NOT in this phase
Client user invites — Phase 2 (need client record first)
Email verification on self-registration — can add later
Team permission management (assigning staff to clients) — Phase 2
Say "proceed" to implement.

-- Phase 1.6: Add invitation token fields to users table
--
-- Why on the users table (not a separate invitations table):
--   An invited user IS their invitation — there's a 1:1 relationship.
--   Embedding the token on the user row avoids an extra table, extra join,
--   and orphan cleanup logic. Once accepted, fields are simply cleared.
--   A separate table becomes worth it when you need multiple pending invites
--   per user or invite audit history — not needed at our scale.

ALTER TABLE "users"
  ADD COLUMN "invitation_token_hash" VARCHAR(64),      -- SHA-256 hex of raw token
  ADD COLUMN "invitation_expires_at" TIMESTAMPTZ;      -- 48h TTL from invite time

-- Index for fast token lookup during acceptance (public endpoint, no RLS context yet)
CREATE INDEX "users_invitation_token_hash_idx"
  ON "users" ("invitation_token_hash")
  WHERE "invitation_token_hash" IS NOT NULL;

import { Injectable, Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
import { ConfigService } from '@nestjs/config';
import * as nodemailer from 'nodemailer';
import * as handlebars from 'handlebars';
import * as fs from 'fs';
import * as path from 'path';

export interface InviteStaffEmailContext {
  agencyName: string;
  inviterName: string;
  inviteeName: string;
  acceptUrl: string;
  expiresInHours: number;
}

export interface WelcomeEmailContext {
  firstName: string;
  agencyName: string;
  loginUrl: string;
}

@Injectable()
export class EmailService {
  private readonly logger = new Logger(EmailService.name);
  private transporter: nodemailer.Transporter;
  private readonly from: string;
  private readonly templateCache = new Map<string, HandlebarsTemplateDelegate>();

  constructor(private readonly config: ConfigService) {
    this.from = this.config.get<string>('email.from')!;

    this.transporter = nodemailer.createTransport({
      host: this.config.get<string>('email.smtp.host'),
      port: this.config.get<number>('email.smtp.port'),
      secure: this.config.get<number>('email.smtp.port') === 465,
      auth: {
        user: this.config.get<string>('email.smtp.user'),
        pass: this.config.get<string>('email.smtp.pass'),
      },
    });
  }

  async sendInviteStaff(to: string, ctx: InviteStaffEmailContext): Promise<void> {
    await this.send(to, `You're invited to join ${ctx.agencyName}`, 'invite-staff', ctx);
  }

  async sendWelcome(to: string, ctx: WelcomeEmailContext): Promise<void> {
    await this.send(to, `Welcome to ${ctx.agencyName}!`, 'welcome', ctx);
  }

  // ─── Private ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  private async send(
    to: string,
    subject: string,
    templateName: string,
    context: Record<string, unknown>,
  ): Promise<void> {
    const html = this.renderTemplate(templateName, context);

    try {
      await this.transporter.sendMail({ from: this.from, to, subject, html });
      this.logger.log(`Email sent: ${templateName} → ${to}`);
    } catch (err) {
      // Log but do NOT throw — a failed email should not fail the HTTP request.
      // The invitation record is already created. The admin can resend.
      this.logger.error(`Failed to send email [${templateName}] to ${to}: ${String(err)}`);
    }
  }

  private renderTemplate(name: string, context: Record<string, unknown>): string {
    if (!this.templateCache.has(name)) {
      const templatePath = path.join(__dirname, 'templates', `${name}.hbs`);
      const source = fs.readFileSync(templatePath, 'utf-8');
      this.templateCache.set(name, handlebars.compile(source));
    }
    return this.templateCache.get(name)!(context);
  }
}

import {
  IsString,
  IsOptional,
  MaxLength,
  MinLength,
  Matches,
  IsUrl,
} from 'class-validator';
import { ApiPropertyOptional } from '@nestjs/swagger';

export class UpdateAgencyDto {
  @ApiPropertyOptional({ example: 'Acme Marketing Agency' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsString()
  @MinLength(2)
  @MaxLength(255)
  name?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({
    example: 'acme-agency',
    description: 'URL-safe slug used for subdomain: <slug>.agencypulse.com',
  })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsString()
  @MinLength(3)
  @MaxLength(100)
  // Only lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens. No leading/trailing hyphens.
  @Matches(/^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+)*$/, {
    message: 'slug must be lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only (e.g. my-agency)',
  })
  slug?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ example: 'https://cdn.example.com/logo.png' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsUrl()
  @MaxLength(500)
  logoUrl?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ example: '#3B82F6', description: 'Brand color (hex)' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsString()
  @Matches(/^#[0-9A-Fa-f]{6}$/, { message: 'primaryColor must be a valid hex color (e.g. #3B82F6)' })
  primaryColor?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ example: 'reports.myagency.com' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsString()
  @MaxLength(255)
  customDomain?: string;
}
Write D:\projects\agency-backend\src\modules\agency\agency.service.ts
56 lines
import {
  Injectable,
  NotFoundException,
  ConflictException,
} from '@nestjs/common';
import { PrismaService } from '../../database/prisma.service';
import { UpdateAgencyDto } from './dto/update-agency.dto';
import { Agency } from '@prisma/client';

@Injectable()
export class AgencyService {
  constructor(private readonly prisma: PrismaService) {}

  async getMyAgency(tenantId: string): Promise<Agency> {
    const agency = await this.prisma.agency.findUnique({
      where: { id: tenantId },
    });

    if (!agency) throw new NotFoundException('Agency not found');
    return agency;
  }

  async updateMyAgency(tenantId: string, dto: UpdateAgencyDto): Promise<Agency> {
    // Check slug uniqueness if being changed
    if (dto.slug) {
      const existing = await this.prisma.agency.findUnique({
        where: { slug: dto.slug },
      });
      if (existing && existing.id !== tenantId) {
        throw new ConflictException('This slug is already taken. Choose a different one.');
      }
    }

    // Check custom domain uniqueness if being changed
    if (dto.customDomain) {
      const existing = await this.prisma.agency.findFirst({
        where: { customDomain: dto.customDomain },
      });
      if (existing && existing.id !== tenantId) {
        throw new ConflictException('This custom domain is already in use.');
      }
    }

    return this.prisma.agency.update({
      where: { id: tenantId },
      data: {
        ...(dto.name && { name: dto.name }),
        ...(dto.slug && { slug: dto.slug }),
        ...(dto.logoUrl !== undefined && { logoUrl: dto.logoUrl }),
        ...(dto.primaryColor !== undefined && { primaryColor: dto.primaryColor }),
        ...(dto.customDomain !== undefined && { customDomain: dto.customDomain }),
      },
    });
  }
}

import { Controller, Get, Patch, Body } from '@nestjs/common';
import { ApiTags, ApiBearerAuth, ApiOperation } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { UserRole } from '@prisma/client';
import { AgencyService } from './agency.service';
import { UpdateAgencyDto } from './dto/update-agency.dto';
import { Roles } from '../auth/decorators/roles.decorator';
import { CurrentUser } from '../auth/decorators/current-user.decorator';
import { AuthenticatedUser } from '../auth/strategies/jwt.strategy';

@ApiTags('Agency')
@ApiBearerAuth()
@Controller('agencies')
export class AgencyController {
  constructor(private readonly agencyService: AgencyService) {}

  @Get('me')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Get own agency details' })
  // AGENCY_ADMIN+ can view (AGENCY_STAFF and CLIENT_USER cannot see agency settings)
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  getMyAgency(@CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser) {
    return this.agencyService.getMyAgency(user.tenantId);
  }

  @Patch('me')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Update agency profile (name, slug, branding)' })
  // Only the AGENCY_OWNER can change the agency profile
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_OWNER)
  updateMyAgency(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Body() dto: UpdateAgencyDto,
  ) {
    return this.agencyService.updateMyAgency(user.tenantId, dto);
  }
}

Write D:\projects\agency-backend\src\modules\team\team.service.ts
243 lines
import {
  Injectable,
  ConflictException,
  NotFoundException,
  BadRequestException,
  ForbiddenException,
} from '@nestjs/common';
import { ConfigService } from '@nestjs/config';
import { UserRole } from '@prisma/client';
import * as crypto from 'crypto';
import { PrismaService } from '../../database/prisma.service';
import { PasswordService } from '../auth/password.service';
import { EmailService } from '../email/email.service';
import { InviteStaffDto } from './dto/invite-staff.dto';
import { AcceptInviteDto } from './dto/accept-invite.dto';
import { ResendInviteDto } from './dto/resend-invite.dto';
import { AuthenticatedUser } from '../auth/strategies/jwt.strategy';

const INVITE_TTL_HOURS = 48;

@Injectable()
export class TeamService {
  constructor(
    private readonly prisma: PrismaService,
    private readonly passwordService: PasswordService,
    private readonly emailService: EmailService,
    private readonly config: ConfigService,
  ) {}

  // ─── List team members ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  async listTeam(tenantId: string) {
    const members = await this.prisma.user.findMany({
      where: {
        tenantId,
        role: { in: [UserRole.AGENCY_OWNER, UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN, UserRole.AGENCY_STAFF] },
        isActive: true,
      },
      select: {
        id: true,
        firstName: true,
        lastName: true,
        email: true,
        role: true,
        isActive: true,
        emailVerifiedAt: true,
        lastLoginAt: true,
        createdAt: true,
        // Never return passwordHash, invitationTokenHash
      },
      orderBy: { createdAt: 'asc' },
    });

    return members;
  }

  // ─── Invite staff ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  async inviteStaff(
    tenantId: string,
    inviter: AuthenticatedUser,
    dto: InviteStaffDto,
  ) {
    // Block duplicate email (globally unique)
    const existing = await this.prisma.user.findUnique({
      where: { email: dto.email.toLowerCase() },
    });
    if (existing) {
      throw new ConflictException('A user with this email already exists.');
    }

    const { rawToken, tokenHash, expiresAt } = this.generateInviteToken();

    // Create the user record — no password until they accept
    const invited = await this.prisma.user.create({
      data: {
        tenantId,
        email: dto.email.toLowerCase(),
        firstName: dto.firstName,
        lastName: dto.lastName,
        role: UserRole.AGENCY_STAFF,
        isActive: false,             // not active until invitation accepted
        invitedById: inviter.userId,
        invitationTokenHash: tokenHash,
        invitationExpiresAt: expiresAt,
      },
    });

    // Load agency name for the email
    const agency = await this.prisma.agency.findUnique({ where: { id: tenantId } });

    const acceptUrl = `${this.config.get('app.url')}/accept-invite?token=${rawToken}`;

    await this.emailService.sendInviteStaff(invited.email, {
      agencyName: agency!.name,
      inviterName: `${inviter.firstName} ${inviter.lastName}`,
      inviteeName: `${dto.firstName} ${dto.lastName}`,
      acceptUrl,
      expiresInHours: INVITE_TTL_HOURS,
    });

    return {
      message: `Invitation sent to ${invited.email}`,
      userId: invited.id,
    };
  }

  // ─── Accept invitation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  // This is a PUBLIC endpoint — no JWT context, no RLS tenant set.
  // We find the user by token hash (no tenant scoping needed here — token is globally unique).

  async acceptInvite(dto: AcceptInviteDto) {
    const tokenHash = this.hashToken(dto.token);

    // Raw SQL via queryRaw to bypass RLS — we have no tenant context yet at this point.
    // The token hash is globally unique so this lookup is safe without tenant scoping.
    const users = await this.prisma.$queryRaw<
      Array<{ id: string; invitation_expires_at: Date | null; email: string; first_name: string; tenant_id: string }>
    >`
      SELECT id, invitation_expires_at, email, first_name, tenant_id
      FROM users
      WHERE invitation_token_hash = ${tokenHash}
        AND is_active = false
      LIMIT 1
    `;

    if (!users.length) {
      throw new BadRequestException('Invalid or already used invitation token.');
    }

    const user = users[0];

    if (!user.invitation_expires_at || user.invitation_expires_at < new Date()) {
      throw new BadRequestException('This invitation has expired. Ask your admin to resend it.');
    }

    const passwordHash = await this.passwordService.hash(dto.password);

    // Activate the user and clear the invitation token
    await this.prisma.$executeRaw`
      UPDATE users
      SET
        password_hash           = ${passwordHash},
        is_active               = true,
        email_verified_at       = NOW(),
        invitation_token_hash   = NULL,
        invitation_expires_at   = NULL,
        updated_at              = NOW()
      WHERE id = ${user.id}::uuid
    `;

    // Load agency for welcome email
    const agency = await this.prisma.$queryRaw<Array<{ name: string }>>`
      SELECT name FROM agencies WHERE id = ${user.tenant_id}::uuid LIMIT 1
    `;

    await this.emailService.sendWelcome(user.email, {
      firstName: user.first_name,
      agencyName: agency[0]?.name ?? '',
      loginUrl: `${this.config.get('app.url')}/login`,
    });

    return { message: 'Invitation accepted. You can now log in.' };
  }

  // ─── Resend invitation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  async resendInvite(tenantId: string, inviter: AuthenticatedUser, dto: ResendInviteDto) {
    const user = await this.prisma.user.findFirst({
      where: { email: dto.email.toLowerCase(), tenantId, isActive: false },
    });

    if (!user) {
      throw new NotFoundException('No pending invitation found for this email in your agency.');
    }

    const { rawToken, tokenHash, expiresAt } = this.generateInviteToken();

    await this.prisma.user.update({
      where: { id: user.id },
      data: {
        invitedById: inviter.userId,
        invitationTokenHash: tokenHash,
        invitationExpiresAt: expiresAt,
      },
    });

    const agency = await this.prisma.agency.findUnique({ where: { id: tenantId } });
    const acceptUrl = `${this.config.get('app.url')}/accept-invite?token=${rawToken}`;

    await this.emailService.sendInviteStaff(user.email, {
      agencyName: agency!.name,
      inviterName: `${inviter.firstName} ${inviter.lastName}`,
      inviteeName: `${user.firstName} ${user.lastName}`,
      acceptUrl,
      expiresInHours: INVITE_TTL_HOURS,
    });

    return { message: `Invitation resent to ${user.email}` };
  }

  // ─── Remove team member ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  async removeTeamMember(tenantId: string, requesterId: string, targetUserId: string) {
    const target = await this.prisma.user.findFirst({
      where: { id: targetUserId, tenantId },
    });

    if (!target) throw new NotFoundException('Team member not found.');

    // Cannot remove yourself
    if (target.id === requesterId) {
      throw new BadRequestException('You cannot remove yourself.');
    }

    // Cannot remove the AGENCY_OWNER — they must transfer ownership first
    if (target.role === UserRole.AGENCY_OWNER) {
      throw new ForbiddenException('Cannot remove the agency owner. Transfer ownership first.');
    }

    // Soft deactivate — preserve audit trail and historical data
    await this.prisma.user.update({
      where: { id: targetUserId },
      data: { isActive: false },
    });

    return { message: 'Team member removed.' };
  }

  // ─── Private helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  private generateInviteToken() {
    const rawToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'); // 64-char hex
    const tokenHash = this.hashToken(rawToken);
    const expiresAt = new Date(Date.now() + INVITE_TTL_HOURS * 60 * 60 * 1000);
    return { rawToken, tokenHash, expiresAt };
  }

  private hashToken(raw: string): string {
    return crypto.createHash('sha256').update(raw).digest('hex');
  }
}
Write D:\projects\agency-backend\src\modules\team\team.controller.ts
66 lines
import {
  Controller,
  Get,
  Post,
  Delete,
  Body,
  Param,
  ParseUUIDPipe,
} from '@nestjs/common';
import { ApiTags, ApiBearerAuth, ApiOperation } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { UserRole } from '@prisma/client';
import { Throttle } from '@nestjs/throttler';
import { TeamService } from './team.service';
import { InviteStaffDto } from './dto/invite-staff.dto';
import { ResendInviteDto } from './dto/resend-invite.dto';
import { Roles } from '../auth/decorators/roles.decorator';
import { CurrentUser } from '../auth/decorators/current-user.decorator';
import { AuthenticatedUser } from '../auth/strategies/jwt.strategy';

@ApiTags('Team')
@ApiBearerAuth()
@Controller('team')
export class TeamController {
  constructor(private readonly teamService: TeamService) {}

  @Get()
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'List all team members (staff + admins)' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  listTeam(@CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser) {
    return this.teamService.listTeam(user.tenantId);
  }

  @Post('invite')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Invite a new staff member' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  // Stricter rate limit on invite — prevents bulk invite spam
  @Throttle({ default: { limit: 20, ttl: 60_000 } })
  inviteStaff(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Body() dto: InviteStaffDto,
  ) {
    return this.teamService.inviteStaff(user.tenantId, user, dto);
  }

  @Post('resend-invite')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Resend invitation email to a pending staff member' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  @Throttle({ default: { limit: 10, ttl: 60_000 } })
  resendInvite(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Body() dto: ResendInviteDto,
  ) {
    return this.teamService.resendInvite(user.tenantId, user, dto);
  }

  @Delete(':userId')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Remove a team member (deactivates account)' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_OWNER)
  removeTeamMember(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Param('userId', ParseUUIDPipe) targetUserId: string,
  ) {
    return this.teamService.removeTeamMember(user.tenantId, user.userId, targetUserId);
  }
}
Write D:\projects\agency-backend\src\modules\team\team.module.ts
17 lines
import { Module } from '@nestjs/common';
import { TeamController } from './team.controller';
import { TeamService } from './team.service';
import { EmailModule } from '../email/email.module';

    EmailModule,
    AuthModule, // imports PasswordService
  ],
  controllers: [TeamController],
  providers: [TeamService],
  exports: [TeamService], // exported so Phase 2 (client invites) can reuse patterns
})
export class TeamModule {}

import {
  Controller,
  Get,
  Post,
  Delete,
  Body,
  Param,
  ParseUUIDPipe,
  HttpCode,
  HttpStatus,
} from '@nestjs/common';
import { ApiTags, ApiBearerAuth, ApiOperation, ApiResponse } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { UserRole } from '@prisma/client';
import { Throttle } from '@nestjs/throttler';
import { TeamService } from './team.service';
import { InviteStaffDto } from './dto/invite-staff.dto';
import { AcceptInviteDto } from './dto/accept-invite.dto';
import { ResendInviteDto } from './dto/resend-invite.dto';
import { Roles } from '../auth/decorators/roles.decorator';
import { Public } from '../auth/decorators/public.decorator';
import { CurrentUser } from '../auth/decorators/current-user.decorator';
import { AuthenticatedUser } from '../auth/strategies/jwt.strategy';

@ApiTags('Team')
@ApiBearerAuth()
@Controller('team')
export class TeamController {
  constructor(private readonly teamService: TeamService) {}

  @Get()
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'List all team members (staff + admins)' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  listTeam(@CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser) {
    return this.teamService.listTeam(user.tenantId);
  }

  @Post('invite')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Invite a new staff member' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  @Throttle({ default: { limit: 20, ttl: 60_000 } })
  inviteStaff(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Body() dto: InviteStaffDto,
  ) {
    return this.teamService.inviteStaff(user.tenantId, user, dto);
  }

  @Post('resend-invite')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Resend invitation email to a pending staff member' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  @Throttle({ default: { limit: 10, ttl: 60_000 } })
  resendInvite(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Body() dto: ResendInviteDto,
  ) {
    return this.teamService.resendInvite(user.tenantId, user, dto);
  }

  // ─── POST /team/accept-invite ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  // Public — no JWT. The invited user has no account yet.
  // Placed here (not in AuthController) to avoid a circular module dependency:
  // TeamModule imports AuthModule (for PasswordService), so AuthModule cannot
  // also import TeamModule.
  @Public()
  @Post('accept-invite')
  @HttpCode(HttpStatus.OK)
  @Throttle({ default: { limit: 10, ttl: 900_000 } })
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Accept invitation and set password' })
  @ApiResponse({ status: 200, description: 'Account activated — you can now log in' })
  @ApiResponse({ status: 400, description: 'Invalid or expired token' })
  acceptInvite(@Body() dto: AcceptInviteDto) {
    return this.teamService.acceptInvite(dto);
  }

  @Delete(':userId')
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Remove a team member (deactivates account)' })
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_OWNER)
  removeTeamMember(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Param('userId', ParseUUIDPipe) targetUserId: string,
  ) {
    return this.teamService.removeTeamMember(user.tenantId, user.userId, targetUserId);
  }
}

  app: {
    nodeEnv: process.env.NODE_ENV || 'development',
    port: parseInt(process.env.PORT || '3000', 10),
    url: process.env.APP_URL || 'http://localhost:5173',       // used in invite links
    frontendUrl: process.env.FRONTEND_URL || 'http://localhost:5173',
  },
  database: {
    url: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
    // Owner role URL — used only by SystemPrismaService for auth bootstrap ops
    migrationUrl: process.env.MIGRATION_DATABASE_URL || process.env.DATABASE_URL,
  },
  redis: {
    host: process.env.REDIS_HOST || 'localhost',
    port: parseInt(process.env.REDIS_PORT || '6379', 10),
    password: process.env.REDIS_PASSWORD,
  },
  jwt: {
    accessSecret: process.env.JWT_ACCESS_SECRET,
    accessExpiresIn: process.env.JWT_ACCESS_EXPIRES_IN || '15m',
    refreshSecret: process.env.JWT_REFRESH_SECRET,
    refreshExpiresIn: process.env.JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRES_IN || '7d',
  },

    key: process.env.ENCRYPTION_KEY,

    secretKey: process.env.STORAGE_SECRET_KEY,
    bucket: process.env.STORAGE_BUCKET || 'agencypulse',
    region: process.env.STORAGE_REGION || 'auto',
  },
});

Phase 2.1 — Updated Plan
DB Changes (one migration)
New table: client_user_assignments


id, tenant_id → agencies,
user_id → users (must be CLIENT_USER role),
client_id → clients,
assigned_by_id → users,
created_at
UNIQUE(user_id, client_id)
INDEX(tenant_id, client_id)
No clientId column on users. ✅

Data Scoping (service layer)
Role	List clients	Get client by ID
AGENCY_OWNER / AGENCY_ADMIN	All non-deleted clients	Any client in tenant
AGENCY_STAFF	Clients in staff_client_assignments for their userId	Only assigned clients
CLIENT_USER	Clients in client_user_assignments for their userId	Only their assigned clients
API Endpoints
Method	Route	Min Role	Notes
POST	/clients	AGENCY_ADMIN	Create client
GET	/clients	CLIENT_USER	List — scoped by role
GET	/clients/:id	CLIENT_USER	Get one — scoped by role
PATCH	/clients/:id	AGENCY_ADMIN	Update
DELETE	/clients/:id	AGENCY_OWNER	Soft delete
POST	/clients/:id/restore	AGENCY_OWNER	Restore soft-deleted
Prisma Schema additions
ClientUserAssignment model (mirrors StaffClientAssignment)
Updated User model — add clientUserAssignments relation

52 lines
-- Phase 2.1: client_user_assignments table
--
-- Design decision: separate assignments table (NOT clientId on users)
-- Reason: a CLIENT_USER may be invited to multiple clients (multiple brands,
-- multiple accounts). A foreign key on users locks them to exactly one client
-- forever — a schema change later would be destructive.
-- This mirrors the existing staff_client_assignments pattern exactly.
--
-- RLS: follows the same pattern as staff_client_assignments —
-- PERMISSIVE policy on tenant_id added in Phase 1.3 migration.

CREATE TABLE "client_user_assignments" (
  "id"             UUID        NOT NULL DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  "tenant_id"      UUID        NOT NULL,
  "user_id"        UUID        NOT NULL,   -- must be CLIENT_USER role
  "client_id"      UUID        NOT NULL,
  "assigned_by_id" UUID        NOT NULL,   -- who created this assignment (audit)
  "created_at"     TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),

  CONSTRAINT "client_user_assignments_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id"),
  CONSTRAINT "client_user_assignments_tenant_id_fkey"
    FOREIGN KEY ("tenant_id") REFERENCES "agencies" ("id") ON DELETE RESTRICT,
  CONSTRAINT "client_user_assignments_user_id_fkey"
    FOREIGN KEY ("user_id") REFERENCES "users" ("id") ON DELETE CASCADE,
  CONSTRAINT "client_user_assignments_client_id_fkey"
    FOREIGN KEY ("client_id") REFERENCES "clients" ("id") ON DELETE CASCADE,
  CONSTRAINT "client_user_assignments_assigned_by_id_fkey"
    FOREIGN KEY ("assigned_by_id") REFERENCES "users" ("id") ON DELETE RESTRICT,
  -- A client user can only be assigned to the same client once
  CONSTRAINT "client_user_assignments_user_id_client_id_key"
    UNIQUE ("user_id", "client_id")
);

-- Find all client users for a given client
CREATE INDEX "client_user_assignments_tenant_id_client_id_idx"
  ON "client_user_assignments" ("tenant_id", "client_id");

-- Find all clients a client user can access
CREATE INDEX "client_user_assignments_user_id_idx"
  ON "client_user_assignments" ("user_id");

-- Enable RLS — policies inherited from Phase 1.3 pattern
ALTER TABLE "client_user_assignments" ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- RLS policy: agencypulse_app can only see rows matching current tenant
-- (matches the PERMISSIVE policy pattern from Phase 1.3)
CREATE POLICY "tenant_isolation_client_user_assignments"
  ON "client_user_assignments"
  FOR ALL
  TO agencypulse_app
  USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)::uuid);

import {
  IsString,
  IsOptional,
  MinLength,
  MaxLength,
  IsUrl,
  Matches,
} from 'class-validator';
import { ApiProperty, ApiPropertyOptional } from '@nestjs/swagger';

export class CreateClientDto {
  @ApiProperty({ example: 'Acme Corp' })
  @IsString()
  @MinLength(1)
  @MaxLength(255)
  name: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ example: 'https://acmecorp.com' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsUrl()
  @MaxLength(500)
  website?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ example: 'https://cdn.example.com/logo.png' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsUrl()
  @MaxLength(500)
  logoUrl?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({
    example: '#10B981',
    description: 'Accent color for this client\'s portal (hex)',
  })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsString()
  @Matches(/^#[0-9A-Fa-f]{6}$/, { message: 'color must be a valid hex color (e.g. #10B981)' })
  color?: string;
}

import { PartialType } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { IsEnum, IsOptional } from 'class-validator';
import { ClientStatus } from '@prisma/client';
import { ApiPropertyOptional } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { CreateClientDto } from './create-client.dto';

export class UpdateClientDto extends PartialType(CreateClientDto) {
  @ApiPropertyOptional({ enum: ClientStatus, example: ClientStatus.INACTIVE })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsEnum(ClientStatus)
  status?: ClientStatus;
}
Write D:\projects\agency-backend\src\modules\clients\dto\client-query.dto.ts
32 lines
import { IsOptional, IsEnum, IsInt, Min, Max, IsString } from 'class-validator';
import { Type } from 'class-transformer';
import { ApiPropertyOptional } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { ClientStatus } from '@prisma/client';

export class ClientQueryDto {
  @ApiPropertyOptional({ enum: ClientStatus, description: 'Filter by status' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsEnum(ClientStatus)
  status?: ClientStatus;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ description: 'Search by name (case-insensitive partial match)' })
  @IsOptional()
  @IsString()
  search?: string;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ default: 1, minimum: 1 })
  @IsOptional()
  @Type(() => Number)
  @IsInt()
  @Min(1)
  page?: number = 1;

  @ApiPropertyOptional({ default: 20, minimum: 1, maximum: 100 })
  @IsOptional()
  @Type(() => Number)
  @IsInt()
  @Min(1)
  @Max(100)
  limit?: number = 20;
}

Write D:\projects\agency-backend\src\modules\clients\clients.service.ts
